title: The Group Chat
theme: Peer exclusion, school life, social comparison

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Take a look around. Most people think loneliness is easy to spot. Someone sitting alone. Someone crying. Someone with nobody around them. Usually, it's not that obvious.

scenes.prompt: Look around the cafeteria. Click on things to observe.

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: One clue doesn't tell you much. But something feels off. Keep looking.

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: Hmm, she hasn't touched her lunch?

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: Maybe. You know what, something feels off. I might not figure it out today, but maybe soon enough?

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: People do interesting things when they don't feel comfortable somewhere. Sometimes they leave. Sometimes they prepare to leave.

dialogue.speaker: player
dialogue.text: And here we go… Suddenly you're the behavioural expert.

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: Ha. Ha. Funny. Joke about it all you want but I ain't all blind.

closing.speaker: narration
closing.text: A loud laugh erupts from another table. Maya instinctively glances over. Just for a second. Nobody notices. The bell rings.

closing.speaker: narration
closing.text: Students immediately begin standing. The cafeteria empties. Maya remains seated. Three seconds. Four. Five. Then she gets up.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: What am I looking for now?

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: Maya steps into the crowd. Alone. Not dramatically alone. Just… nobody walking beside her. She pulls out her phone.

pause_dialogue.speaker: player
pause_dialogue.text: So why's she staring at it like it's a maths exam?

pause_dialogue.speaker: avery
pause_dialogue.text: Maybe because saying yes is easy.

scenes.prompt: Which answer sounds like what she wants to say?

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: Notice she didn't actually say yes.

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: That's usually why people say maybe.

outcome.insight: Vague answers can be a way of avoiding both commitment and rejection.

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: I think she answered a little too quickly.

outcome.insight: Declining quickly can sometimes be a way of protecting yourself from a situation that feels unsafe.

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: Because suddenly it's not a hangout anymore.

outcome.insight: Being reminded that everyone is included except you can make an invitation feel like a reminder of exclusion.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: Maya arrives home. The room is tidy but unfinished. A moving box sits beside her desk. A photo from her old school is pinned to the wall. Half-unpacked books rest on a shelf.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: She tosses her backpack onto the floor and drops onto her bed.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: How long ago did she move?

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Long enough to stop being the new kid.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: Doesn't really look like she settled in though.

intro_dialogue.speaker: avery
intro_dialogue.text: And here comes the evening routine.

intro_dialogue.speaker: avery
intro_dialogue.text: Wait until you see how exciting mine is.

observation_dialogue.speaker: player
observation_dialogue.text: That's a lot of people having fun.

observation_dialogue.speaker: avery
observation_dialogue.text: Social media's basically a highlight reel.

dialogue.speaker: player
dialogue.text: She isn't doing anything. No comments. No messages. No posts. She's just… watching?

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: Ever stand outside a party and hear everyone inside laughing?

dialogue.speaker: avery
dialogue.text: I don't know. But it kinda feels like she's on the outside of something.

closing_dialogue.speaker: narration
closing_dialogue.text: Maya opens her messages. No new notifications. She closes them again.

closing_dialogue.speaker: player
closing_dialogue.text: That one felt a little harder to explain away.

closing_dialogue.speaker: avery
closing_dialogue.text: Exactly. The photo could've meant nothing. The lunch could've meant nothing. The way she sat could've meant nothing. But all together...

closing_dialogue.speaker: player
closing_dialogue.text: They're starting to look like a pattern.

closing_dialogue.speaker: avery
closing_dialogue.text: Let's take a closer look. Maybe there's something we've missed.

scenes.title: Signal Detection
scenes.instructions: Maya's social feed is full of clues. Identify what each post or behaviour signals about her emotional state.

signals.statement: She liked the mall photo but wasn't in it.
signals.explanation: Engaging with content that excludes you is a subtle sign of wanting to belong while feeling left out.

signals.statement: She scrolls through social media every evening but never posts or comments.
signals.explanation: Passive consumption without engagement can signal feeling disconnected — present online, but not really part of anything.

signals.statement: She smiled only when Preethi texted her.
signals.explanation: Even one genuine connection can be a meaningful anchor. Preethi seems to be that person for Maya.

signals.statement: She gave a vague 'maybe' instead of saying yes or no to the invitation.
signals.explanation: Vague responses to social invitations can reflect uncertainty about belonging — wanting to connect but not feeling safe enough to commit.

signals.statement: Eight months later, her room is still half-unpacked.
signals.explanation: Physical signs like an unfinished living space can reflect emotional rootlessness — not fully settling because it doesn't yet feel like home.

scenes.title: Chapter 1 Complete
scenes.summary: Maya looks socially connected on the surface — she has classmates, a phone full of apps, and at least one friend texting her. But the small things told a different story. The untouched lunch. The half-packed room. The scrolling without connecting. Loneliness isn't always loud.
keyLearnings: Loneliness can exist even when someone is surrounded by people.
Behavioral cues — posture, phone habits, eating — can reveal emotional states.
Social media use without interaction can be a sign of disconnection.
One genuine connection (like Preethi) can matter more than many surface-level ones.
